convo.text: Should we use Postgres or MongoDB for an early B2B SaaS primary datastore? Give me a recommendation and your reasoning.

**Recommendation: Postgres.** For an early-stage B2B SaaS primary datastore, this isn't close. Reach for MongoDB only if you have a specific, identified reason — and "we might need flexibility" isn't one.

## Why Postgres wins here

**1. B2B data is relational, and you can't predict the joins yet.**
B2B SaaS is accounts → users → roles → subscriptions → invoices → audit logs → resource hierarchies. That's a graph of relationships with referential integrity that actually matters (you don't want an invoice pointing at a deleted org). Postgres enforces this with foreign keys and gives you arbitrary joins for free. In MongoDB you either embed (and fight duplication/update anomalies) or reference (and reimplement joins in application code). Early on you *don't know* your access patterns yet — and Mongo's document model makes you commit to them up front, while Postgres lets you query any shape after the fact.

**2. Transactions and correctness.**
Billing, seat counts, plan changes, usage metering — B2B is full of multi-row invariants where a partial write is a real bug that costs you money or trust. Postgres gives you battle-tested ACID transactions as the default. Mongo has multi-document transactions now, but they're a bolt-on with performance caveats, not the path of least resistance.

**3. You get the "flexibility" argument anyway.**
The historical case for Mongo was schemaless/JSON. Postgres has `jsonb` with GIN indexing — so for the genuinely unstructured parts (per-tenant custom fields, event payloads, settings blobs) you store documents *and* keep relational integrity for everything else. You rarely need to choose.

**4. It scales further than people claim, and you won't hit the wall early.**
A single Postgres instance handles a very large B2B workload (B2B has far fewer rows than B2C — thousands of orgs, not hundreds of millions of consumers). You also get a clean tenant-isolation story (row-level security, or schema-per-tenant) baked in.

**5. Ecosystem leverage when you're small and time-poor.**
Every ORM, every analytics tool, every BI/warehouse connector, managed offerings (RDS/Aurora, Cloud SQL, Supabase, Neon), and the broadest hiring pool all treat Postgres as a first-class citizen. As an early team this operational maturity is worth more than any raw feature.

## When MongoDB would actually be the right call
- Your core domain is genuinely document-shaped with no cross-entity integrity needs (e.g. a CMS, product catalog, flexible config store as the *whole* product).
- Deeply nested, variable-schema documents are your primary read/write unit and you almost never join across them.
- You need its specific horizontal-sharding model from day one — rare for early B2B.

Even then, many teams run Postgres as the system of record and add a document/search store later for that one use case. Adding specialization later is easy; retrofitting relational integrity and transactions onto a document store is painful.

## Bottom line
Start with **Postgres** as your single primary datastore. Use `jsonb` for the flexible columns. Defer any second datastore until a concrete workload (full-text search, analytics, caching) earns it. The biggest early-stage risk isn't picking the "wrong" scalable database — it's spending complexity budget solving scale problems you don't have yet, and Postgres lets you avoid that the longest.

One caveat worth naming: this is the general case. If you tell me your actual domain (what the product does, expected tenant/row counts, any heavy unstructured or search-heavy workloads), I can pressure-test whether the default holds for you.

## Why Postgres wins here

**1. B2B data is relational, and you can't predict the joins yet.**

Billing, seat counts, plan changes, usage metering — B2B is full of multi-row invariants where a partial write is a real bug that costs money or trust. Postgres gives you battle-tested ACID transactions by default. Mongo has multi-document transactions now, but they're a bolt-on with performance caveats, not the path of least resistance.

**3. You get the "flexibility" argument anyway.**
The historical case for Mongo was schemaless JSON. Postgres has `jsonb` with GIN indexing, so for the genuinely unstructured parts (per-tenant custom fields, event payloads, settings blobs) you store documents *and* keep relational integrity for everything else. You rarely have to choose.

**4. It scales further than people claim, and you won't hit the wall early.**
A single Postgres instance handles a very large B2B workload — B2B has far fewer rows than B2C (thousands of orgs, not hundreds of millions of consumers). You also get a clean tenant-isolation story (row-level security, or schema-per-tenant) built in.

## When MongoDB would be the right call
- Your core domain is genuinely document-shaped with no cross-entity integrity needs (a CMS, product catalog, or flexible config store as the *whole* product).

- You need its specific horizontal-sharding model from day one — rare for early B2B.

Even then, most teams run Postgres as the system of record and add a document/search store later for that one use case. Adding specialization later is easy; retrofitting integrity and transactions onto a document store is painful.

## Final answer
**Go with Postgres as your single primary datastore.** Use `jsonb` for the flexible columns, and defer any second datastore until a concrete workload (full-text search, analytics, caching) earns it. The biggest early-stage risk isn't picking the "wrong" scalable database — it's burning your complexity budget on scale problems you don't have yet, and Postgres lets you avoid that the longest. The general case and your case point the same way here: start on Postgres, and only revisit if a specific document-shaped workload later proves otherwise.
Should we use Postgres or MongoDB for an early B2B SaaS primary datastore? Give me a recommendation and your reasoning.

Use Postgres. For an early B2B SaaS primary datastore, it's the right default, and the bar for choosing MongoDB instead should be a specific data-shape problem you can already name — not a guess about future scale.

Here's the reasoning.

**B2B data is relational, and you'll feel it early.** B2B almost always means accounts containing users, with roles, permissions, subscriptions, invoices, audit logs, and cross-entity reporting. Those are joins and foreign keys. Postgres enforces that structure for you with real constraints; in MongoDB you either denormalize (and then fight update anomalies and consistency bugs by hand) or you reimplement joins in application code. Early on, your schema is changing constantly and your team is small — having the database catch referential mistakes is worth a lot.

**Transactions and integrity are non-negotiable for B2B.** Billing, seat counts, plan changes, and usage metering need to be correct. Postgres gives you mature multi-row ACID transactions, foreign keys, and constraints as first-class, battle-tested features. MongoDB has multi-document transactions now, but they're less central to its design and carry more caveats — you don't want correctness of money and access control resting on the less-trodden path.

**It covers the cases people reach for MongoDB to solve.** "We have flexible/unstructured data" is the usual reason to pick Mongo. Postgres `jsonb` handles schemaless documents with indexing and rich querying inside the same database — so you can keep one system for both your relational core and your flexible blobs. You get document flexibility where you need it without giving up relational guarantees everywhere else.

**Operational and ecosystem maturity reduce risk when you're small.** Every managed provider runs it (RDS/Aurora, Cloud SQL, Supabase, Neon, Crunchy), every ORM and BI/analytics tool speaks SQL natively, hiring is easier, and your investors' and customers' security reviews won't blink. For a small team, "boring and well-supported" is a feature.

**You won't outgrow it on the axis you're worried about.** The instinct to pick Mongo is usually about future scale. Postgres serves very large SaaS companies; you will hit product-market-fit problems, billing edge cases, and reporting demands long before you hit a wall that Postgres can't handle with normal indexing, read replicas, or partitioning.

When MongoDB would actually be the better call: your *primary* workload is genuinely document-centric with deeply variable, nested shapes and few cross-entity relationships — for example a CMS, a product catalog with wildly heterogeneous attributes, event/telemetry ingestion, or a content store. If that's your core domain, Mongo's model fits. But that's a secondary-store or specialized-product situation, not the typical B2B SaaS system of record. If you have both needs, start on Postgres and add a specialized store later when the specific pain is concrete.

Net: default to Postgres now, and only override it if you can point to a real document-shaped core workload today.
